name: Preeclampsia Warning
description: Preeclampsia is a serious blood pressure condition that can happen after the 20th week of pregnancy or after giving birth.
advice: URGENT: This can be a sign of preeclampsia, a serious condition. Contact your healthcare provider or go to the emergency room immediately.
red_flags: Severe headache that won't go away with medication
Changes in vision, including temporary loss of vision, blurred vision or light sensitivity
Pain in your upper belly, usually under your ribs on the right side

name: Decreased Fetal Movement
description: A noticeable decrease in your baby's movements can sometimes indicate that they are distressed.
advice: Drink something cold and sugary, lie on your left side in a quiet room, and do a kick count. If you feel fewer than 10 movements in 2 hours, contact your doctor or go to L&D immediately.
red_flags: A sudden, drastic change in movement patterns

name: Vaginal Bleeding
description: While light spotting can sometimes be normal, especially early on, bleeding can indicate miscarriage, ectopic pregnancy, placental abruption, or placenta previa.
advice: Contact your doctor immediately. Put on a pad to monitor the amount of bleeding.
red_flags: Bleeding that is heavy enough to soak through a pad
Bleeding accompanied by pain or cramping

name: Preterm Labor Contractions
keywords: lower back pain comes and goes
description: Regular contractions that cause your cervix to open before 37 weeks of pregnancy.
advice: Empty your bladder, drink 2-3 large glasses of water, and lie down on your left side for an hour. If you have more than 4-6 contractions in an hour, call your doctor.
red_flags: Contractions that get closer together, stronger, and longer

name: Leaking Amniotic Fluid
description: The sac holding the baby breaks or leaks before labor starts.
advice: Put on a pad to monitor the fluid. Do not use a tampon. Call your healthcare provider or go to the hospital.
red_flags: Fluid is green, yellow, or brown (sign of meconium)
Accompanied by fever or decreased fetal movement

name: Morning Sickness
description: Nausea and vomiting during pregnancy, often triggered by hormonal changes. Despite the name, it can happen at any time of day.
advice:
1. Eat small, frequent meals.
2. Keep crackers by your bed and eat a few before getting up.
3. Try ginger tea, candies, or supplements.
4. Ask your doctor about Vitamin B6 and Unisom.
red_flags: Unable to keep fluids down for 24 hours

name: Fatigue
description: Extreme tiredness caused by a massive increase in progesterone levels, increased blood production, and the physical demands of growing a baby.
advice:
1. Listen to your body and rest when you need to.
2. Go to bed earlier.
3. Eat iron-rich foods to prevent anemia.
4. Delegate tasks when possible.
red_flags: Fatigue accompanied by severe shortness of breath

name: Frequent Urination
description: In the 1st trimester, increased blood flow to the kidneys causes it. In the 3rd trimester, the baby's head presses on the bladder.
advice:
1. Lean forward when you pee to empty your bladder completely.
2. Limit caffeine.
3. Stop drinking fluids a couple of hours before bed, but stay hydrated during the day.
red_flags: Pain or burning when you pee (sign of UTI)
Urine has a strong, foul odor

name: Breast Changes
description: Hormones cause breasts to grow, become tender, and prepare for milk production. Veins may become more prominent and areolas may darken.
advice:
1. Wear a supportive, well-fitting bra without underwire.
2. Use a sleep bra at night for extra support.
3. Apply a warm or cool compress for pain relief.
red_flags: Redness, warmth, and fever (signs of mastitis)

name: Food Cravings and Aversions
description: Hormonal shifts can cause you to strongly desire certain foods or feel repulsed by foods you usually like.
advice:
1. Give in to cravings in moderation, as long as the food is safe for pregnancy.
2. Focus on eating whatever sounds palatable if you have strong aversions.
3. Sneak nutrients into foods you can tolerate (e.g., spinach in a smoothie).
red_flags: Craving non-food items like dirt, clay, or chalk (Pica - tell your doctor)
Severe weight loss due to aversions

name: Round Ligament Pain
description: Sharp, sudden pain in the lower belly or groin caused by the stretching of the ligaments that support the growing uterus.
advice:
1. Move slowly, especially when changing positions.
2. Bend and flex your hips when you sneeze or cough to reduce the pull.
3. A warm (not hot) bath can help relax the muscles.
4. Consider a maternity support belt.
red_flags: Pain that doesn't go away after a few minutes of rest
Pain accompanied by bleeding, cramping, or fever
Severe pain that makes it hard to walk

name: Sciatica
description: Pain radiating from the lower back down the back of the leg, caused by the expanding uterus or baby pressing on the sciatic nerve.
advice:
1. Sleep on your side with a pillow between your knees.
2. Try prenatal yoga or gentle stretching.
3. Use a warm compress on the lower back or buttocks.
4. See a physical therapist or chiropractor experienced in pregnancy.
red_flags: Loss of feeling in your leg or foot
Loss of bowel or bladder control

name: Pelvic Girdle Pain (SPD)
description: Pain in the pubic joint or pelvic region caused by the hormone relaxin making the pelvic joints too loose or unstable.
advice:
1. Keep your knees together when getting in/out of the car or bed.
2. Avoid pushing heavy objects or standing on one leg.
3. Wear a pelvic support belt.
4. Ask for a referral to a pelvic floor physical therapist.
red_flags: Pain so severe you cannot walk
Signs of a urinary tract infection

name: Backaches
description: Caused by weight gain, changes in posture as the baby grows, and hormones relaxing the ligaments in the joints of your pelvis.
advice:
1. Practice good posture and wear supportive shoes.
2. Lift with your legs, not your back.
3. Get a prenatal massage or use a heating pad on the lowest setting.
4. Sleep on your side with a pregnancy pillow.
red_flags: Rhythmic back pain that comes and goes (could be preterm labor)
Severe back pain accompanied by fever or bleeding

name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
description: Swelling and fluid retention during pregnancy can put pressure on the median nerve in the wrist, causing numbness and pain in the hands.
advice:
1. Wear wrist splints, especially at night.
2. Try to keep your wrists straight while sleeping or typing.
3. Shake out your hands or soak them in cold water to reduce swelling.
4. Take frequent breaks if doing repetitive hand motions.
red_flags: Severe pain that wakes you up and isn't relieved by splints
Loss of muscle in the base of the thumb

name: Heartburn and Indigestion
description: Pregnancy hormones relax the valve between the stomach and esophagus, allowing acid to leak up. Later, the growing baby pushes stomach acid upward.
advice:
1. Eat smaller, more frequent meals.
2. Avoid spicy, greasy, or highly acidic foods.
3. Don't lie down right after eating; wait at least 2 hours.
4. Ask your doctor about safe antacids (like Tums).
red_flags: Severe chest pain that doesn't feel like normal heartburn

name: Constipation
description: An increase in progesterone slows down the digestive system to extract more nutrients for the baby. Iron supplements can also contribute.
advice:
1. Drink plenty of water (8-12 cups a day).
2. Increase fiber intake (fruits, vegetables, whole grains).
3. Get regular, gentle exercise like walking.
4. Ask your doctor about a safe stool softener or switching iron supplements.
red_flags: No bowel movement for several days despite trying remedies

name: Bloating and Gas
description: Increased progesterone relaxes the smooth muscle tissue in your body, including your gastrointestinal tract, which slows digestion and leads to bloating and gas.
advice:
1. Eat slowly and chew your food thoroughly.
2. Avoid foods that typically cause you gas (e.g., beans, cabbage, carbonated drinks).
3. Gentle exercise can help move gas through your system.
4. Eat smaller, more frequent meals.
red_flags: Diarrhea or vomiting that won't stop

name: Hemorrhoids
description: Swollen veins in the rectum or anus caused by the pressure of the growing uterus and increased blood volume. Often worsened by constipation.
advice:
1. Prevent constipation by eating fiber and drinking water.
2. Don't strain during bowel movements.
3. Soak in a warm bath (sitz bath) or use witch hazel pads.
4. Ask your doctor about safe topical treatments.
red_flags: Severe pain that makes it impossible to sit
A hard lump that is very painful

name: Metallic Taste (Dysgeusia)
description: A common early pregnancy symptom caused by the surge of estrogen, which can affect your sense of taste.
advice:
1. Try eating citrus fruits or drinking lemonade to cut the metallic taste.
2. Use plastic silverware instead of metal.
3. Brush your tongue when you brush your teeth.
4. Chew sugarless gum or suck on hard candies.
red_flags: Usually harmless, but mention it to your doctor if it prevents you from eating or drinking entirely.

name: Linea Nigra
description: A dark vertical line that appears down the center of your belly due to increased melanin production from pregnancy hormones.
advice: Use sunscreen, wear a hat, and avoid excessive sun exposure. It usually fades postpartum.

name: Stretch Marks
description: Red, pink, or purple streaks that appear on the belly, breasts, or thighs as the skin stretches to accommodate the growing baby.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: PUPPP Rash
description: Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy. An intensely itchy rash that usually starts in stretch marks on the belly in the 3rd trimester.
advice: Use oatmeal baths, aloe vera, or unscented moisturizers. Ask your doctor about safe anti-itch creams or antihistamines.

name: Melasma (Mask of Pregnancy)
description: Dark, blotchy patches that appear on the face (usually forehead, cheeks, upper lip) due to hormonal changes and sun exposure.
advice: Use sunscreen, wear a hat, and avoid excessive sun exposure. It usually fades postpartum.

name: Itchy Skin
description: Common on the stretching belly. However, severe itching, especially on palms and soles without a rash, needs checking.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.
red_flags: Intense itching on the palms of hands and soles of feet (especially at night) - could be Cholestasis, a liver condition.

name: Acne
description: Hormonal surges can increase sebum (oil) production, leading to breakouts, especially in the 1st trimester.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Hair Growth/Loss
description: Hormones can make scalp hair thicker and slow down shedding. You might also notice more hair on your belly or face.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Nail Changes
description: Pregnancy hormones can make nails grow faster, but for some, they become more brittle or develop grooves.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Skin Tags
description: Small, harmless flaps of skin that often appear in areas where skin rubs together, like the neck, armpits, or groin.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Spider Veins
description: Tiny, red or purple blood vessels that appear on the face, neck, or legs due to increased blood volume and hormones.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Vivid Dreams
description: Hormonal fluctuations, interrupted sleep, and processing the major life change of having a baby can cause extremely vivid or strange dreams.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Pregnancy Brain
description: A common feeling of forgetfulness or lack of focus, likely related to fatigue, hormone changes, and stress.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Mood Swings
description: Rapid changes in mood caused by fluctuating hormones, physical discomfort, and the emotional impact of impending parenthood.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Lightheadedness
description: Caused by changes in circulation; your blood vessels dilate, and blood pressure drops. Later on, the baby can press on major blood vessels.
advice: Get up slowly from sitting or lying down. Eat regularly to keep blood sugar stable. Don't stand for long periods.

name: Headaches
description: Common in the 1st trimester due to hormones and blood volume changes. Can also be caused by caffeine withdrawal or dehydration.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.
red_flags: Severe headache that won't go away
Headache accompanied by vision changes or swelling (signs of preeclampsia)

name: Anxiety
description: Normal to feel anxious about the baby's health, labor, or parenting. If it becomes overwhelming, it needs addressing.
advice: Talk to your doctor, a therapist, or a support group. Treatment options are available and safe during pregnancy.
red_flags: Thoughts of harming yourself or the baby
Inability to function in daily life

name: Depression
description: Not just "baby blues." Persistent feelings of sadness, worthlessness, or lack of interest in things you usually enjoy.
advice: Talk to your doctor, a therapist, or a support group. Treatment options are available and safe during pregnancy.
red_flags: Thoughts of harming yourself or the baby
Inability to function in daily life

name: Restless Leg Syndrome
description: An irresistible urge to move the legs, often accompanied by a crawling or tingling sensation, usually worse at night.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Insomnia
description: Difficulty falling or staying asleep due to physical discomfort, frequent urination, anxiety, or vivid dreams.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Hot Flashes
description: Sudden feelings of intense heat, often accompanied by sweating, caused by hormonal fluctuations and increased metabolism.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Braxton Hicks
description: Practice contractions where the uterus tightens and relaxes. They prepare your body for labor but don't dilate the cervix.
advice: Change positions, drink water, or take a warm bath. They should stop or become less frequent.
red_flags: Contractions become regular, stronger, and closer together (real labor)

name: Lightning Crotch
description: Sudden, sharp, shooting pain in the pelvis, vagina, or rectum. Often caused by the baby's head pressing on nerves as they drop lower.
advice: Change positions, wear a supportive belly band, and try to rest.

name: Nesting Instinct
description: A sudden burst of energy and an intense urge to clean, organize, and prepare your home for the baby's arrival.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Colostrum Leakage
description: Your breasts may start producing and leaking colostrum, the nutrient-rich first milk, weeks or months before birth.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Baby Dropping (Lightening)
description: When the baby settles deeper into your pelvis in preparation for birth. Breathing may get easier, but pelvic pressure increases.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Mucus Plug Loss
description: Passing the thick plug of mucus that sealed the cervix. It can be clear, pink, or slightly bloody. It means the cervix is changing.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Pelvic Pressure
keywords: feels like baby will fall out
description: A feeling of heaviness or pressure in the pelvis or vagina, caused by the baby's weight as they move lower.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Swollen Feet and Ankles
description: Mild swelling is very common due to extra fluid in the body and pressure from the uterus on veins returning blood from the legs.
advice: Elevate your feet when sitting. Wear comfortable shoes. Drink plenty of water.
red_flags: Sudden or severe swelling in hands, face, or around eyes (preeclampsia)
Swelling in only one leg accompanied by pain or redness (blood clot)

name: Shortness of Breath
description: The growing uterus pushes up against your diaphragm, giving your lungs less room to expand. Usually improves when the baby drops.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Clumsiness
description: Changes in your center of gravity and the hormone relaxin loosening your joints can make you feel more unsteady on your feet.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Nosebleeds
description: Increased blood volume and hormonal changes can make the delicate blood vessels in your nose swell and break more easily.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Stuffy Nose (Rhinitis)
description: Hormones cause the mucous membranes in your nose to swell and produce more mucus, leading to a constant stuffy feeling.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Bleeding Gums
description: Pregnancy hormones make your gums more sensitive to the bacteria in plaque, leading to inflammation and bleeding.
advice: Use a soft-bristled toothbrush. Floss gently daily. Keep your regular dental checkups.

name: Increased Saliva (Ptyalism)
description: An excessive production of saliva, often associated with severe morning sickness or heartburn. Can be quite bothersome.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Vaginal Discharge (Leukorrhea)
description: An increase in thin, white, milky, and mild-smelling discharge is normal and helps prevent infections traveling from the vagina to the womb.
advice: Wear breathable cotton underwear. Use a panty liner if needed. Do not douche.
red_flags: Discharge that is green, yellow, foul-smelling, or accompanied by itching/burning (signs of infection)

name: Leg Cramps
description: Painful, involuntary muscle contractions, usually in the calves, that often happen at night. The exact cause is unclear but may be related to weight or circulation.
advice: Stretch your calf muscle before bed. If a cramp hits, flex your foot upward (toes toward your nose). Stay hydrated.

name: Snoring
description: Swollen nasal passages and weight gain can lead to snoring, even if you've never snored before.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Vision Changes
description: Fluid retention can slightly change the shape of your cornea, making your vision blurry or making contacts uncomfortable.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Changes in Pigmentation
description: Increased melanin production can cause areolas, freckles, and moles to darken. Always have new or changing moles checked.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.

name: Heart Palpitations
description: Your heart has to work harder and pump more blood. Occasional palpitations are normal, but frequent or sustained ones should be checked.
advice: Rest, stay hydrated, and mention it to your doctor at your next visit.
